skill.name: code-formatter
skill.description: Formats multi-language code using project configurations and established formatter conventions.
skill.summary: Apply consistent formatting across common programming languages while respecting existing project settings.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged a Markdown usage block as shell execution and two text lines as weak cryptography. Manual review found these are false positives with no executable code, prompt injection, network access, data exfiltration, or real cryptographic logic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Usage Example
low_findings.description: The detected backtick block is a fenced Markdown usage example for invoking the skill. It is not Ruby or shell execution, and it does not interpolate user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is only a Markdown code fence containing example invocations. No executable script or shell command runner appears in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The line contains the skill description for code formatting. No cryptographic function, hash algorithm, encryption call, or credential handling is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static match has no semantic connection to cryptography. The line is plain metadata describing formatting behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The line references popular style guides for formatting decisions. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, weak algorithm selection, or sensitive data handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line discusses code style guidance, not security primitives. There is no cryptographic implementation to evaluate.

content.value_statement: Inconsistent formatting slows reviews and hides meaningful code changes. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply project-aware formatting across common languages.
actual_capabilities: Detects common programming and markup languages in a project or path.
Checks for existing formatter configuration files before applying defaults.
Uses established tools such as Prettier, Black, gofmt, rustfmt, and Google Java Format.
Applies documented spacing, line length, semicolon, quote, and trailing comma preferences.
Reports formatted files and explains style decisions after changes.
Skips generated code, vendor directories, binary files, and lock files.
limitations: Requires compatible formatter tools to be available in the working environment.
Does not include custom formatting rules beyond the documented guidance.
Should ask before modifying project configuration files.
May need user direction for unusual languages or mixed formatting conventions.

use_cases.title: Prepare a pull request
use_cases.description: Format changed files before review so diffs focus on behavior and content.

use_cases.title: Standardize a mixed codebase
use_cases.description: Apply consistent formatting across JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust, Java, HTML, CSS, JSON, YAML, and Markdown.

use_cases.title: Check formatting without changes
use_cases.description: Review formatting issues before deciding whether to update files.

prompt_templates.title: Format the current project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-formatter to format the current project. Respect existing configuration files and summarize every file changed.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a full project format pass.

prompt_templates.title: Format one folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-formatter on the src directory only. Skip generated files, vendor folders, binary files, and lock files.
prompt_templates.scenario: Targeted formatting for a specific source directory.

prompt_templates.title: Run a check-only review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-formatter in check-only mode. Report files that need formatting and explain which formatter or style rule applies.

prompt_templates.title: Apply language-specific formatting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-formatter for Python files only. Prefer existing pyproject.toml settings, otherwise use Black-compatible formatting and PEP 8 guidance.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with language and configuration constraints.

output_examples.input: Format a JavaScript and Markdown project before review.
output_examples.output: A concise report lists formatted JavaScript and Markdown files, notes the Prettier settings used, and identifies skipped lock files.

output_examples.input: Check formatting for a Python package without editing files.
output_examples.output: A check-only summary identifies files that need formatting and explains whether Black, autopep8, or project configuration would apply.

output_examples.input: Format a Go service and avoid vendor content.
output_examples.output: The result reports gofmt changes for source files and confirms that vendor directories, generated files, and lock files were skipped.

best_practices: Run the formatter on a clean working tree so changes are easy to review.
Keep project formatter configuration files under version control.
Use check-only mode in continuous integration before enforcing formatting changes.
anti_patterns: Do not format generated code, vendor directories, binary files, or lock files.
Do not rewrite formatter configuration files without explicit approval.
Do not mix broad formatting changes with unrelated feature edits.

faq.question: Which languages does this skill support?
faq.answer: It covers JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Go, Rust, Java, CSS, HTML, JSON, YAML, and Markdown.

faq.question: Does it respect existing project settings?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks for files such as .prettierrc, .editorconfig, and pyproject.toml before using defaults.

faq.question: Can it run without changing files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented check-only mode reports formatting issues before edits are made.

faq.question: Will it format generated or vendor files?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs the agent to skip generated code, vendor directories, binary files, and lock files.

faq.question: Does it install formatter tools?
faq.answer: No. It assumes required tools such as Prettier, Black, gofmt, or rustfmt are available when needed.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found only documentation and formatting instructions, with no confirmed dangerous behavior.
